coredata.dc:title: An imine COF as a dual-functional fluorescent sensing platform for vitamin K1 and water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Shili
Gao, Yu
Wang, Dan
You, Xingyu
Zhao, Fuquan
Chu, Hongtao
Zhao, Ming
Jin, Fenglong
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent covalent organic frameworks (LCOFs) with large π-conjugated system were a family of crystalline porous materials with promising applications. However, the actual application and quenching mechanisms of the LCOFs with dual-functionality remain poor understood. Here, a micro-mesoporous luminescent covalent organic framework (LCOF-DT) with high crystallinity and good stability was prepared by the solvothermal method using 1, 3, 5-tri-(4-aminophenyl) benzene and 2, 5-dihydroxyterephthalaldehyde as monomers through Schiff base condensation reaction. The LCOF-DT exhibited not only a good sensitivity and colorimetric visualization for water in conventional organic solvents over a relatively broad linear range (0 %–50 %) but also showed exceptional fluorescent recognition capability for vitamin K1, even in the presence of 17 different competing substrates. Additionally, a sensitive, specific, rapid, and practical fluorescent method for vitamin K1 detection in the commercially vitamin K1 tablets was developed. Based on the aforementioned research, designing covalent organic frameworks with multifunctional fluorescent response would be a tendency in the further application of detection technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent covalent organic frameworks
Dual-functionality
Fluorescent probe
Vitamin K1
Water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent detection of water and VK1
Comparison of LCOF-DT to other LCOFs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support of the Fundamental Research Funds (Hemp Special Project) of Heilongjiang Provincial Education Department (No. 145409433).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD characterization of LCOF-DT (the inset represented a localized enlargement in the range of 2–10°).
The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isothermal curves (a); pore size distribution diagram of LCOF-DT (b).
The effects of excitation wavelength (a) and standing time (b) on fluorescence intensity of LCOF-DT.

The chemical stability of LCOF-DT including (a) the time stability of fluorescence intensity in various solvents, (b) the crystalline stability in different solvents and (c) the pH-dependent stability of fluorescence intensity.

The effect of water content on fluorescence intensity of LCOF-DT in DMF(a); EtOH(b); MeOH(c); ACN(d) (the illustration showed the linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity F/F0 and different contents of water).
Color changes of LCOF-DT in DMF (a), MeOH (b), EtOH (c) and ACN (d) with increasing water content (0 %–100 %).
The selectivity and anti-interference of LCOF-DT.
Fluorescence spectra of LCOF-DT quenched by different concentrations of VK1 (a) and Stern-Volmer quenching plots for VK1 (b).

UV absorption spectra of LCOF-DT, VK1 and their mixtures (a); UV absorption spectrum of VK1 and emission spectrum of LCOF-DT (b); Zeta potential of LCOF-DT, VK1 and their mixtures (c); Fluorescent decay lifetime (d).

head.ce:title: An imine COF as a dual-functional fluorescent sensing platform for vitamin K1 and water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp, State Administration for Market Regulation, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp, State Administration for Market Regulation, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qiqihar Inspection and Testing Center, Qiqihar Administration for Market Regulation, Qiqihar 161000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qiqihar Inspection and Testing Center, Qiqihar Administration for Market Regulation, Qiqihar 161000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, PR China (Ming Zhao).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent covalent organic frameworks (LCOFs) with large π-conjugated system were a family of crystalline porous materials with promising applications. However, the actual application and quenching mechanisms of the LCOFs with dual-functionality remain poor understood. Here, a micro-mesoporous luminescent covalent organic framework (LCOF-DT) with high crystallinity and good stability was prepared by the solvothermal method using 1, 3, 5-tri-(4-aminophenyl) benzene and 2, 5-dihydroxyterephthalaldehyde as monomers through Schiff base condensation reaction. The LCOF-DT exhibited not only a good sensitivity and colorimetric visualization for water in conventional organic solvents over a relatively broad linear range (0 %–50 %) but also showed exceptional fluorescent recognition capability for vitamin K1, even in the presence of 17 different competing substrates. Additionally, a sensitive, specific, rapid, and practical fluorescent method for vitamin K1 detection in the commercially vitamin K1 tablets was developed. Based on the aforementioned research, designing covalent organic frameworks with multifunctional fluorescent response would be a tendency in the further application of detection technology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) represent a compelling category of covalent organic polymers composed of lightweight elements such as C, H, N, O, F and S, which were first characterized by presence dynamic covalent bonds, structural long-range order, and crystallinity in 2005 [1]. Recent researches underscore the benefits of COFs including low-density ordered channels, large specific surface area, highly tunable porosity, diverse building blocks, abundant functional groups, and tailored structures [2]. These characteristics contribute to their outstanding performance in applications such as gas storage [3], adsorption [4] and separation, catalysis and the other related fields [5,6].

Meanwhile, luminescent covalent organic framework (LCOFs) as an emerging new class of COFs can be designed by extending π-conjugation or introducing quantum dot in their backbone resulting in useful photoluminescence properties [7,8]. LCOFs exhibited potential abilities as fluorescent probes and have been reviewed by previous research [9,10]. Li et al. synthesized five 1, 3, 5-tris(phenylacetylene)- benzene-based LCOFs emitted blue, green, yellow, orange and red light depending on the conjugation degree caused by the effect of the different linkers, which could be successfully used to construct proto-typed white LED [11]. A covalent organic framework with bidentate ligand sites was designed and synthesized for the detection of Cu2+ in environmental water with high sensitivity and selectivity, which integrated multi-functionality such as extended π-conjugation framework, tunable functionality, regular pore structure, specific sites to interact with targets as well as robust chemical and thermal stability [12]. Furthermore, polyimide covalent organic nanosheets, azine-linkage COF and COF-HQ were all used as fluorescent sensors in quantitative detection experiments of nitro aromatic compounds [13], heavy ions [14] and pH [15]. Furthermore, the LCOF of a flexible alkylamine COF was applied for detection and removal of mercury [16], and the phenylboronic acid functionalized LCOF was applied for sensing and adsorption of rifamycin antibiotics [17].

The good selectivity and large adsorption capacity make LCOFs more and more appealing scientists [18–21]. Among the numerous LCOFs, imine-based LCOFs were noteworthy, which had better wettable and chemical stability, stronger fluorescence intensity than the boron-based COF even though it showed relative low crystallinity [22,23]. LCOF Dha Tab (LCOF-DT) was firstly synthesized using 2, 5-dihydroxyphthalaldehyde (Dha) and 1, 3, 5-tri(4-aminophenyl) benzene (Tab) as monomers through Schiff base reaction. Although LCOF-DT demonstrated excellent properties [24], few studies have further explored its applications, particularly in the context of dual-functional LCOFs that exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity in detecting two compounds.

Vitamin K1 (VK1, also called phylloquinone) is the main natural occurring form of vitamin K, which is a generic term for liposoluble compounds with methylated naphthoquinone structure [25]. Several works have shown a direct correlation between low levels of vitamin K and deterioration of cognitive and behavior performances [26]. Therefore, the quantification of VK1 in food, health care products and human body is of considerable importance. Currently, the quantification of VK1 commonly employs the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method [27]. Compared with method, fluorescent analysis has been considered an excellent method for VK1 detection because of its simplicity, real-time monitoring, quick response and sensitivity. Nevertheless, the background interference within complex matrices prevents fluorescence method from further application for VK1 detection [27–29]. The development of fluorescent probe with good selectivity and specific anti-interference ability is necessary. Meanwhile, LCOFs have been applied for the determination of water content in organic solvents in recent years [30,31]. These studies concentrated on the presence of trace amounts of water in organic solvents, typically constituting less than 10 % of the solvent composition [30–34]. However, the extensive range of water content and the associated variations in chromaticity during detection have not been reported in the literature.

In this study, we have developed a dual-functional sensing platform utilizing LCOF-DT, which exhibited high sensitivity and strong selectivity. This platform facilitated the rapid detection of VK1 in health supplement tablets and the determination of water content in four organic solvents. The synthesis of LCOF-DT was successfully accomplished using a solvothermal approach, as confirmed by multiple characterization techniques. Furthermore, LCOF-DT exhibited exceptional selectivity for VK1 over 17 potential interfering substances and demonstrated high sensitivity, with a detection limit of 2.18 μmol/L. Moreover, it effectively quantified the water content in organic solvents, such as N, N-dimethylformamide, acetonitrile, ethanol, and methanol, across a wide range of 0 % to 50 % water content.

Tab and Dha were obtained from Shanghai Tengqian Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). O-dichlorobenzene (o-DCB) was provided from Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-butanol (n-BuOH), ultra-dry tetrahydrofuran, anhydrous ethanol (EtOH), acetic acid, anhydrous N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), anhydrous methanol (MeOH), anhydrous ethyl acetate (EA) and anhydrous acetonitrile (ACN) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 10 kinds of vitamins compounds (K1, B5, K2, B1, C, A, D2, D3, E and B2) and the spectroscopic grade quinine sulphate were purchased from Innochem Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 2 kinds of bioorganic molecules and 6 kinds of biologically relevant ions (L-cysteine, glucose, NH4 +, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and Cu2+) different from biological samples were obtained from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The other solvents and reagents were of analytical grade. All water used in the experiment was ultrapure water.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected using the Bruker D8 diffractometer (Karlsruhe, Germany), scanning at an angle of 0°–40°. Surface morphology and element distribution were obtained by an S-4300 scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy experiment was obtained using an FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of the materials were examined on a surface area analyzer (Conta, USA) at 77 K. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) data were collected with a TA Instrument (Hitachi, Japan). Zeta-potential (Zeta) data were obtained through a Nano ZS 90 zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, UK). Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra and quantum yields of LCOF-DT were analyzed using a LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) and UV-1901 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Persee, China).

The synthesis of LCOF-DT conducted with slight modifications from the procedure reported in the literature [35]. Specifically, 56.2 mg of Tab and 39.9 mg of Dha were precisely weighed and dissolved in 6.0 mL of o-DCB and n-BuOH mixed solution (v/v = 1/1). Subsequently, 0.6 mL of 0.6 mol/L acetic acid was added, and the mixture was thoroughly homogenized. The solution was then transferred to a 25 mL reactor and subjected to temperature of 120 °C for a duration of 72 h. Post-reaction, the resulting product was washed five times with THF and EtOH to yield a bright yellow precipitate, which was dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight. The resultant yield of the brick-red precipitate, following the completion of five replicate experiments, ranged from 88 % to 91 %.

The quantum yield (QYs) of LCOF-DT was determined by the relative method using quinine sulphate as standard [36]. The quinine sulphate dissolved 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 and LCOF-DT suspensoid were prepared, and their absorbance were recorded. After that, the fluorescence intensity of the same solutions was recorded upon excitation at 305 nm, and QYs was calculated using the following equation.
Q
Y S
= Q
Y
St
η S 2
η
St
2
F S
A S
A
St
F
St

In the formula, the integral area of the fluorescence emission peak (F); the UV absorbance of the material (A); ƞ represents the refractive index of the solvent. Subscript s and st represented the sample and the standard [37].

The effects of excitation wavelength (290–320 nm), standing time (0.1–24.0 h), the type of disperse solvent (MeOH, EtOH, DMF, EA, ACN and H2O) and the pH value adjusted by acetic acid or triethanolamine (4.0–9.0) on the fluorescence intensity of LCOF-DT were investigated at a concentration of 7 μg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of water and VK1
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, four organic solvents including DMF, MeOH, EtOH, and ACN were selected as analytes to assess the water content in organic solvents using LCOF-DT fluorescent probe. 1.0 mL of the target analyte solution, with water content ranging from 2 % to 100 %, was mixed with 1.0 mL 14.0μg mL−1 LCOF-DT suspension in a quartz cuvette. The resulting diluted target analytes exhibited water content ranging from 1 % to 50 %. Emission spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 305 nm using a fluorescence spectrometer.

Secondly, 10 kinds of vitamins (B5, B1, C, B2, K1, K2, A, D2, D3, and E) and 2 kinds of bioorganic molecules and 6 kinds of biologically relevant ions (L-cysteine, glucose, NH4 +, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and Cu2+) were selected to measure the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the fluorescent probe. The suspension dispersed with DMF of 1.0 mL 10.0 mmol/L analytes and 1.0 mL 14.0 μg L−1 LCOF-DT was mixed in a quartz cuvette, and the diluted target analyte was measured using an LS55 fluorescence spectrometer to evaluate the selectivity. Then, the interference resistance experiment was measured at an excitation wavelength of 305 nm after adding 100 μL of the solution containing 10−3 mol/L the other interfering substance to mixed solution of 2.0 mL LCOF-DT + K1. In addition, the influence of response time on the fluorescence quenching of LCOF-DT by VK1 was investigated, ranging from 0.5 to 3.0 min.

The quenching constants were calculated using Stern-Volmer, F and F0 represented the fluorescence emission intensity of LCOF-DT solution in the presence and absence of analytes, respectively, Ksv is the quenching constant, and C is the concentration of analytes.
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
sv
C

The experimental data were represented in the form of standard deviation for 10 parallel measurements. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined as follows: δ and K represented the standard deviation and the slope, respectively.

LOD =
3 δ
K

Finally, the quantitative method of VK1 in commercially available VK1 tablets was developed and validated using LCOF-DT as fluorescent probe. The pretreatment procedure for the actual tablet samples involved the following steps: an accurately weighed 20 mg portion of the crushed test sample was placed into a 50 mL volumetric flask and dissolved using DMF solvent. The solution was then centrifugated at 4000 r/min for 10 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane for fluorescence analysis. Furthermore, the accuracy was validated by the recovery test through standard addition method. The spiked concentrations were 3.0 µmol/L, 15.0 µmol/L and 85.0 µmol/L, respectively.
In addition, the same actual tablet samples were processed using the methods specified in the 2020 edition of the Chinese Pharmacopoeia, and the VK1 content was determined and analyzed.

LCOF-DT was prepared under solvothermal conditions via Schiff base condensation using Dha and Tab as the predesigned precursors, with acetic acid serving as a catalyst, as illustrated in Fig. S1. The reaction mechanism involved a nucleophilic addition reaction between an amino amine compound (Tab) and an aldehyde or ketone compound (Dha) containing a carbonyl group, facilitated by the affinity reagent acetic acid. In this process, the lone pair of electrons on Tab attacked the carbon atom with positive charge of the carbonyl group on Dha to complete the affinity addition reaction, forming α-hydroxyl amine compound, and further dehydration condensation to form Schiff base [38].

To demonstrate the successful synthesis of LCOF-DT, a comprehensive suite of characterization methods such as SEM, XRD, FT-IR, nitrogen adsorption–desorption and TGA were used to elucidate the morphology, structural characteristics and properties of the synthesized material.

Firstly, it was observed that LCOF-DT showed a slight agglomeration and regular spherical morphology with rough surface and a particle size of about 0.5 μm as shown in Fig. 1 . Secondly, the high crystallinity of LCOF-DT was proved by XRD shown in Fig. 2 . The strong peak at 2.7° and the small peaks at 4.8°, 5.6° and 7.5° of XRD spectrum correspond to 100, 110, 200 and 120 crystal planes in LCOF structure, respectively [30,39]. The high crystallinity of LCOF-DT was attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds (O-H∙∙∙∙N = C) between hydroxyl and imine groups, which not only facilitated the generation of LCOF-DT with a high degree of crystallinity, but also immobilized the phenyl ring in the same plane and enhanced the stacking of the COF layers [40]. Thirdly, the characteristic functional groups of LCOF-DT were analyzed by FT-IR and showed in Fig. S2. The N–H stretching vibration peaks of primary amine groups were appeared 3425 cm−1 and 3349 cm−1. The characteristic absorption peaks of benzene ring were at 1610 cm−1, 1500 cm−1 and 1440 cm−1. It is noteworthy that, the newly observed absorption peak at 1587 cm−1 has been attributed to the stretching vibration of the imine bond (CN). This observation proved the occurrence of the acetal reaction process between the functional groups of Dha and Tab.

Furthermore, the results of nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiment showed that the adsorption isotherm of LCOF-DT belonged to type IV adsorption isotherm with H1 hysteresis loop, which was one of the main characteristics of micro-mesoporous structure and uniform spherical particles in Fig. 3 (a) and (b). According to Brunauer-Emmett-Teller and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda method analysis, LCOF-DT exhibited a specific surface area of 931.15 m2/g and pore sizes of 2.19 nm and 3.82 nm, respectively. Finally, LCOF-DT exhibited superior thermal stability according to Fig. S3. The maximum weight loss temperature of LCOF-DT took place at about 405 °C, which was caused by the decomposition and collapse of COF skeleton and it completely disaggregated at 1000℃.

To sum up, LCOF-DT prepared in this experiment had the regular spherical morphology, the high crystallinity, the abundant functional groups, the large specific surface area, the uniform micro-mesoporous structures and the superior thermal stability, which would further benefit its applications.

The luminescent properties of LCOF-DT were studied detailedly including the excitation wavelength, fluorescence quantum yield (QYs ), the influence of the type of disperse solvent, the chemical stability of LCOF-DT.

As illustrated in Fig. 4 (a), the emission wavelength of LCOF-DT was consistently observed at 425 nm for the different excitation wavelength in range of 290 nm to 320 nm. The maximum fluorescence emission intensity was recorded at 425 nm with an excitation wavelength of 305 nm, indicating that the optimal excitation wavelength for LCOF-DT was 305 nm. The QYs of LCOF-DT was determined by comparison with quinine sulfate, which served as the reference substance (QYs = 54 %) [41]. The QYs value of LCOF-DT was found to be 5.64 %, as presented in Table S1.

Notably, an interesting phenomenon was observed by investigating the effects of different disperse solvents on fluorescence intensity of LCOF-DT as shown in Fig. 4(b). The highest fluorescence emission intensity was recorded when DMF was used as the dispersing solvent among five solvents tested. A relatively strong fluorescence emission band was also detected for LCOF-DT in ACN, EtOH, EA and MeOH as shown in Fig. S4. In contrast, when dispersed in H2O, LCOF-DT exhibited negligible luminescence and formed a homogeneous dark brown suspension. This behavior indicated the distinctive selectivity of LCOF-DT towards H2O, suggesting its potential application as a fluorescent sensor for water detection.

The chemical stability of LCOF were indeed crucial for assessing the usefulness and reliability. So the time stability of fluorescence intensity in various solvents, the crystalline stability in different solvents, the pH-dependent stability of fluorescence intensity in DMF solvent besides the thermal stability were further investigated.

The variation in fluorescence intensity of LCOF-DT was assessed in five organic solvents ranging from 0.1 to 24 h, as illustrated in Fig. 5 (a). This result indicated that LCOF-DT showed the relative high fluorescence response in the various solvents, which lasted for up to 24 h. The fluorescence intensity exhibited a slight reduction within the range of 0.36 % to 0.80 %, suggesting that LCOF-DT possessed the commendable time stability of fluorescence intensity in five solvents. Furthermore, the crystalline stability of LCOF-DT in five different solvents was assessed by XRD characterization. As shown in Fig. 5(b) the characteristic diffraction peaks of LCOF-DT at 2.7°, 4.8°, 5.6°, and 7.5° were retained, albeit with slight broadening of the peak shapes. This result indicated that LCOF-DT exhibited the substantial crystalline stability following exposure to different solvent treatments. In addition, the pH-dependent stability of fluorescence intensity in DMF solvent was examined. the result demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of LCOF-DT exhibited relative minimal variation in a wide pH range (4–9) in Fig. 5(c), indicating the high stability and the good resistance to both acidic and alkaline conditions.
The above results demonstrated that the prepared LCOF-DT not only showed higher QYs but also exhibited the superior chemical stability compared to the other imine-linked LCOF materials [42–44].

The content of water in organic solvent was quantitatively determined using LCOF-DT as a fluorescent probe based the above experiments. Four organic solvents (DMF, EtOH, MeOH and ACN) that could be mixed with water in any proportion were selected as the analytes, and the fluorescence spectra of LCOF-DT in four solvent systems with different water contents were measured.

It can be seen from Fig. 6 . that with the increase of water content, the fluorescence intensity of LCOF-DT in the four solvent systems gradually decreased, and the fluorescence emission peak centers were red-shifted. This phenomenon was attributable to the hydrogen bonding interaction. Firstly, an increase in water content could enhance the hydrogen bonding interaction between the organic solvent and water, thereby intensifying the quenching effect due to the expansion of LCOF-DT [45]. Secondly, the hydrogen bond interactions between the hydroxyl groups of water molecules and the imine functional groups of LCOF-DT facilitated the release of rotation and vibration of the benzene ring, leading to quenching [46]. Simultaneously, the fluorescence spectra demonstrated a significant red shift in the emission peak of LCOF-DT with increasing water content. This phenomenon can be ascribed to the diminished dispersion of LCOF-DT in water, leading to its progressive aggregation [46].

The fat-soluble compounds were selected as the analytes to assess the fluorescent selectivity of LCOF-DT, due to the weak fluorescence intensity exhibited by LCOF-DT in aqueous environments. As illustrated in the blue bar chart of Fig. 8 , the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0 ) of LCOF-DT was most significantly quenched by VK1, while other vitamins (K2, A, D2, D3, and E) exhibited negligible fluorescence quenching. Meanwhile, considering the applicability domain of VK1 and its biological significance, four water-soluble vitamins (B5, B1, C and B2), two bioorganic molecules (L-cysteine, glucose) and six biologically relevant ions (NH4 +, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+) were also examined. The results, presented in the blue bar chart of Fig. 8, demonstrated a high selectivity for VK1.

Furthermore, the anti-interference performance of the fluorescent probe was evaluated shown in the red bar chart of Fig. 8. When an equivalent amount of VK1 with these competitive substrates, no significant spectral changes or variations in emission intensity were observed in the probe-VK1-interference matrices mixture. This indicated that LCOF-DT exhibited a favorable anti-interference capability, suggesting its applicability in practical sample analyses.

To further elucidate the practicability of LCOF-DT the effect of response time on F/F0 was investigated in range of 0–3.0 min. As shown in Fig. S5, with the increase of contact time, the F/F0 values of LCOF-DT quenched by VK1 dramatically decreased first and then gradually stabilized, and the fluorescence intensity hardly changed after 1.0 min. It indicated that LCOF-DT could achieve the rapid detection of VK1.

The study examined the quenching effect of VK1 concentration on the fluorescence intensity of LCOF-DT, as depicted in Fig. 9 . A notable quenching was observed with increasing VK1 concentration within the range of 0 to 100 μmol/L, without any accompanying red or blue shift in the emission wavelength. The fluorescence quenching data were further analyzed using the conventional Stern-Volmer equation to quantitatively determine VK1. The calculated KSV was determined to be 5.40 × 103 L/mol, which meant the strong binding ability of fluorescent substance and quencher [47]. The correlation coefficient and the LOD were estimated to be 0.9962 and 2.18 μmol/L, respectively.

Furthermore, the recovery experiments were conducted using the commercially available VK1 tablets as the actual sample to validate the accuracy of the developed fluorescent method for quantitatively determining VK1. The three additional concentration levels were selected to be near the LOD value (3.00 μmol/L), twice the test value (15.0 μmol/L), and close to the upper limit of detection value (85.0 μmol/L). The results showed that the recoveries were between 95.33 % and 103.66 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 5.12 % in Tab S2. Based on the developed fluorescent method, the concentration of VK1 identified in the actual sample tablets was 97.52 ± 2.03 %, which was also assessed using method the 2020 edition of the Chinese Pharmacopoeia, yielding a result of 97.64 ± 1.04 %. These findings align with the drug content specifications outlined in the product instructions and conform to the standards set forth by the 2020 edition of the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (97.0 %–103.0 %). The results indicate that the developed fluorescence method is both accurate and reliable, rendering it suitable for the analysis of commercially available VK1 tablets.

Understanding the mechanism of fluorescence quenching is essential for comprehending the properties of fluorescent analytes and probes. Typically, the quenching mechanism mainly includes internal filtering effect (IFE) [48], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [49] and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [50]. UV–vis, fluorescence spectrum and Zeta potential analysis were used to investigate the quenching mechanism of LCOF-DT caused by VK1.

As shown in Fig. 10 (a), the maximum absorption peaks of the mixture of LCOF-DT and VK1 remained unchanged compared with the UV spectrum of the analytes, and no new absorption peaks were observed, this suggested that there was absence of any coordination in the exited state of LCOF-DT and VK1, no complex was formed, thus PET [51] was excluded. Meanwhile, the excitation and emission wavelengths of the probe were 305 nm and 425 nm, respectively. The UV–Vis absorption spectral band width of VK1 was observed in the range of 290 to 370 nm, with a maximum absorption wavelength at 325 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 10(b). This absorption range partially overlapped with the excitation wavelength, which indicated that the quenching mechanism was FRET or IFE, the luminescent intensity of LCOF-DT decreased due to VK1 absorbing a portion of the probe's fluorescence.

However, one of three conditions for the occurrence of FRET [52] was that the central distance between LCOF-DT as the donor and VK1 as the acceptor should be less than 10 nm [53]. The Zeta potential analysis, as illustrated in Fig. 10(c), revealed that LCOF-DT and VK1 exhibited identical charge valence states, resulting in significant electrostatic repulsion that increased the molecular distance between them.

Meanwhile, for FRET it was believed that that dynamic quenching dominated in the quenching process. However, the fluorescent lifetime of LCOF-DT only reduced 0.0746 ns after adding VK1 shown in Fig. 10(d) and Tab. S3. Furthermore, according to Ksv and the fluorescent lifetime (τ 0) the quenched rate constant (Kq) was calculated and exhibited 3.98 × 1012 L/mol s−1, which was higher than the maximum scatter collision quenched constant (2.0 × 1010 L/mol s−1) [54]. All these findings confirmed that the fluorescence mechanism was predominantly governed by static quenching based on IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of LCOF-DT to other LCOFs
ce:section.ce:para: We have summarized the literature reports on the application of fluorescent probes using LCOFs for the detection of VK1 and water content in organic solvents shown in Tabs. S4 and S5. Despite the discrepancy in LODs of water content between this study and existing researches, LCOF-DT proved to be compatible with a variety of solvents and capable of detecting a broad concentration of water contents to meet specified requirements. It also facilitated distinct color changes that were visible to the naked eye. Additionally, there were no researches on the detection of VK1 using LCOF as the probe. The as-synthesized LCOF-DT with dual-functionality exhibited high sensitivity (LOD = 2.18 μmol/L) and rapid detection (1.0 min) for VK1 and was applied in actual samples.

In conclusion, a dual-functional LCOF-DT has been successfully synthesized through Schiff base polycondensation, which exhibited the regular spherical morphology with size of 0.5 μm, high crystallinity, a large surface area of 931.15 m2/g, a micro-mesoporous structure, and impressive QYs of 5.64 %. Meanwhile, the fluorescent probe demonstrated the rapid and sensitivity to water, enabling effective detection of water content across a broad range (0 %–50 %) in solvents such as DMF, ACN, EtOH, and MeOH. It also exhibited distinct color changes visible to the naked eye at varying water concentrations. Furthermore, LCOF-DT was developed for the selective and sensitive detection of VK1 in the commercially available VK1 tablets, utilizing the static quenching based on IFE mechanism, with a detection limit of 2.18 μ mol/L. Considering its simplicity, low cost, and favorable optical properties, LCOF-DT holds potential for applications in analytical chemistry, bioimaging, and water content detection.

Shili Qin: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yu Gao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Dan Wang: Investigation. Xingyu You: Visualization. Fuquan Zhao: Software, Validation. Hongtao Chu: Software, Validation. Ming Zhao: Supervision. Fenglong Jin: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support of the Fundamental Research Funds (Hemp Special Project) of Heilongjiang Provincial Education Department (No. 145409433).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126110.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
